questions.answer: AD 2022-03-07 is superseded by this AD.
AD 2022-03-07 partially superseded AD 2020-05-11, and this AD fully supersedes AD 2020-05-11.
The regulation body that mandated FAA-AD-2024-01-02 is the FAA (Federal Aviation Administration).
FAA AD 2024-01-02 applies to the Boeing 737-8.
The Boeing 737 MAX is manufactured by The Boeing Company.
The Boeing 737 MAX is equipped with the CFM International LEAP-1B engine.

MCAS Software v2.0 is a mandated safety modification and certified software update that replaces the flawed v1.0. It represents a significant technological upgrade, primarily through the implementation of a Kalman-filter-based sensor fusion algorithm.
Southwest Airlines, American Airlines, and United Airlines are the primary US operators of Boeing 737 MAX aircraft. Ryanair, Air Canada, Norwegian Air Shuttle, and Lion Air also operate the aircraft.
AD 2024-01-02 supersedes AD 2022-03-07. AD 2022-03-07 partially superseded AD 2020-05-11 and is fully superseded by AD 2024-01-02. AD 2020-05-11 is also superseded by AD 2024-01-02.
The required maintenance procedure is a Visual Inspection of the forward engine mount, as specified in AMM Task 71-21-05-200-801, with a compliance interval of every 600 flight cycles or 12 months.
The context does not specify which airlines are affected by MCAS software requirements.

The context does not contain information describing a regulatory path from the FAA to a maintenance crew for the CFM LEAP-1B engine. It only identifies the FAA as the primary certification authority and mentions that Boeing holds FAA Production Approval and Design Approval Holder status under 14 CFR Parts 21, 25, and 39.

The context does not mention Southwest Airlines. It states that fleet-wide, FAA AD 2020-05-11 and FAA AD 2022-03-07 are compliant on all 87 aircraft. FAA AD 2024-01-02 has a compliance deadline of May 15, 2024, with software updates scheduled from February to May 2024. The current software on all 87 aircraft is MCAS v1.7.3, and the target software is MCAS v2.0.

The context does not contain information about a 2022 directive, nor does it describe any relationship between a 2022 directive and the 2020 or 2024 directives. The context only states that compliance is mandatory for all Part 121 operators prior to revenue service resumption, effective May 27, 2020. Therefore, it is not possible to answer how the 2022 directive relates to the others or what it means for current operators.
FAA AD 2020-05-11 is COMPLIANT for all 87 aircraft, with pre-service compliance completed in November 2020. All original compliance requirements have been met and maintained.

The documents state that operators of Boeing 737-8 and 737-9 aircraft are legally required to comply with all applicable Airworthiness Directives (ADs) under 14 CFR Part 39. Non-compliance results in the aircraft being considered unairworthy for operation under FAA jurisdiction. Specific ADs mentioned include FAA AD 2020-05-11, FAA AD 2022-03-07, and FAA AD 2024-01-02.
The context does not contain information about a document that currently governs engine mount inspection for the Boeing 737 MAX.
The context states that AD 2020-05-11 is a previous airworthiness directive that is superseded. Therefore, it is not still the governing airworthiness directive.

The effective regulatory hierarchy for Boeing 737 MAX engine inspections is primarily governed by the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) Airworthiness Directive (AD) 2020-05-11. This directive mandates specific inspections, including those of the Angle of Attack (AOA) sensor brackets and the activation of the AOA Disagree alert. The FAA AD 2020-05-11 serves as the foundational regulatory requirement, outlining the necessary procedures and timelines for compliance.

The context does not contain enough information to answer. The context references AD 2020-05-11 as being superseded by AD 2022-03-07, but it does not mention AD 2024-01-02 or any relationship between AD 2024-01-02 and AD 2020-05-11.

Yes, if Southwest Airlines operates the Boeing 737 MAX, they are subject to FAA-AD-2024-01-02. The Airworthiness Directive applies to the Boeing 737 MAX and its MCAS system, and operators of this aircraft type are required to comply. As an operator of the Boeing 737 MAX, Southwest Airlines would fall under the same regulatory requirement.

The context does not mention FAA AD 2024-01-02. It only states that the A320neo family is subject to EASA AD authority and lists key ADs, but no specific AD numbers are provided. Therefore, it cannot be determined from the context whether FAA AD 2024-01-02 applies to the Airbus A320neo.

No. The context states that the Boeing 737 MAX is a Boeing product and that its sole engine option is the CFM International LEAP-1B. There is no information in the context indicating that Airbus manufactures the Boeing 737 MAX.
Yes, there is an FAA airworthiness directive governing Airbus aircraft in this corpus. Specifically, the corpus references FAA AD 2024-01-02, which applies to the A320neo family of aircraft.

MCAS Software Version 2.0.

AD 2022-03-07 is superseded by this AD. AD 2020-05-11 is partially superseded by AD 2022-03-07 and fully superseded by this AD. The context does not provide the exact document ID of the current directive.

No. The context shows that all 87 aircraft in the MAX 8 fleet are compliant with FAA AD 2020-05-11 and FAA AD 2022-03-07, and all currently have MCAS v1.7.3 installed. There is no evidence of conflicting compliance status for any specific aircraft.

The FAA issued an Emergency Order of Airworthiness on March 13, 2019, grounding all 737-8 and 737-9 aircraft. This was followed by FAA AD 2020-05-11, which established return-to-service requirements. The context does not provide a specific regulatory path from the FAA to a maintenance crew servicing the CFM LEAP-1B engine.

14 CFR Part 39 governs Airworthiness Directives (ADs). The directive that references it is § 39.11 of the Code of Federal Regulations.
The EASA directive covers the Airbus A320neo family, including A319neo, A321neo, and A321XLR variants.
